dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond to 1623 King Street for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain and difficulty breathing. It started maybe this afternoon.
Can you tell me your name and where you are?
My name is Katherine. I'm in [CITY].
Good to hear. What year is it today?
Katherine, can you explain what happened today?
I started having chest pain and I couldn't breathe well.
Can you describe the chest pain? Is it a sharp pain or dull?
It feels like a rock in my chest. Eight out of ten.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Did anything happen before the pain started?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: She said the group home wouldn't let her call 911, so she walked here.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Was the person you used to call 911 from where you were staying at the time of the pain?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No. Not where I live. I live in a group home. They wouldn't let me call.
Let's check your breathing. Can you take a deep breath for me?
Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles noted. Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Pulse ox is at 97% on room air.
Pulse is 87 and regular.
Blood pressure is 179 over 94. Skin is warm and dry, no cyanosis observed.
Breathing is 18 per minute, regular respirations.
SpO₂ is 97% on room air and normal for the patient's baseline.
Blood pressure is 179 over 94, and the pulse is 87.
I'm going to place the ECG pads on you. It's just for monitoring purposes.
Performing 4-lead monitoring. Sinus rhythm identified, no arrhythmias detected.
Some signs of left atrial enlargement noted on rhythm strip from 4-lead ECG.
Applying a 12-lead. Normal sinus rhythm again, no acute ischemic changes evident.
We are checking your blood sugar now with a finger stick.
Blood glucose level is 0-results questionable, likely failed testing challenge or hypoglycemia due to timing or test strip issue.
Katherine, can you tell me what medications you take on a regular basis?
I take Lorazepam, Tavist, Zolpidem, and Zoloft on a regular schedule.
Do you have any known allergies that we should consider at this time?
I'm allergic to lithium; have had a documented adverse reaction in the past.
Let me confirm breath sounds in both lungs quickly for symmetry and clarity.
Lung sounds are clear, equal bilaterally-clear to auscultation-no additional adventitious or abnormal sounds.
Based on the presence of chest pain and elevated BP, we'll follow the chest pain suspected protocol with continuous monitoring now in place for 4-lead and time to 12-lead ECG.
Pulse decreased slightly to 85 now but is still a regular rhythm pattern.
Breathing rate down to 12 per minute now but still steady and regular effort with no use of accessory muscles.
Blood pressure is now 146 over 92 after sitting for a moment, trending downward from the initial reading.
SpO₂ slightly dropped to 95% on room air post-assessment rounds, nothing alarming or requiring intervention at this point.
Rechecking ECG through 4-lead again-still normal sinus rhythm, no ST segment or T-wave elevation or depressions noted so far on strip.
We're preparing to transport to [COMPANY_NAME]. Any preference for destination, Katherine?
That's fine with me.
We'll assist you carefully into the ambulance now. Please gently lie down on the cot for safe transport.
Doors are secure and we are ready to move to the hospital with proper restraint applied for safety procedures in-motion transport.
Giving the nurse a full report now including EKG findings, vitals at 17:09 with the deterioration parameters and chief concern for chest discomfort.
GCS remains 14 (Eye 4, Verbal 4, Motor 6), A&Ox2, no changes in lung function observed to any degree for over 15 minutes with rechecks interspersed per protocol guidelines.
Upon arrival, patient data including initial 12-lead findings from 17:04 have been transmitted digitally to the attending staff with paper tracing in hand.

Patient moved from cot to bed with minimal assistance and minor repositioning. No critical incidents observed in equipment or personnel-has been seen to slide slightly and informed receiving team about the observation.

Your breathing appears stable and breathing is fine at this point for transport conditions with no signs of distress or respiratory compromise during transit to date. We manage potential changes with routine checks already in place with vitals and ECG constantly monitored during all phases of transportation operation here from field, via to hospital.

Transporting now at a safe speed with road traffic conditions on control as professional judgment based on patient needs and higher priority cases pending in back onResponse trunk dispatch reports have been deferred for now.

EKG still in NSR with no additional transient changes detected on strip all that time. No emergent signs modeled from 17:09 milestone processed through 12-lead interpretation until last check at 17:15 noted. Everything up to this point remains NSR pattern check.
Respiration remains on schedule with 12 per minute, both sides getting equal tidal volume and exhalation demonstrated by patient. No resistance or retractions observed on routine scan.

Oxygen saturation remains consistent for room air reading and expected baseline for this sternal level, though it has decayed from 97% to 95% during transport-still within safe normative range for this phase as no alert flags or interventions are necessary at this juncture according to clinical judgment.
We're approaching the ED lot. Please remain still as we prepare for transfer offloading.

Medical transport is complete; all digital patient data uploads have reached the receiving hospital's network as if in initial request from field for transfer setup. No physical printouts forgotten or lost for tracking record.

All initial vitals, ECG readings at 16:59 and 17:04 confirmed and summarized in report note and systematically cross-referenced during onboarding for ED review with charting. Clear document line by line available for access on facility as needed after initial upload and localized office filing after our arrival.

Breath sounds confirmed normal, chest CTAB (clear to auscultation bilaterally), SpO₂ remaining at 95% limit acceptable for triage behaviors observed to receive routine care levels attention here at current setup done via rolling telehealth from ongoing monitoring traces.

Airway still clear and is patent, lungs again clear to auscultation, equal, no murmurs besides baseline ones already known for CTAB - chest intact without trauma evidence underlying pathology concerns via current hand-off strategies setup at this stage, to be taken up by medical staff at ED for priority handling tomorrow.

4-lead monitor still reporting NSR with no acute cardiac changes sensed at 17:15 assessment time-heart rhythm stable for downward travel phase: flatlay category derivative clinical analysis satisfies standard of care here.

12-lead ECG read unchanged as before, no ST segment elevation or T-wave notches noted-which would otherwise indicate MI signs or any such indicator. So condition again overall ruled out for heart attack visuals initially on this very same pass.

We will document the medications she provided for inclusion into medical chart while EMT crew handles the audiovisual component stream for digital archiving if she had preferred to turn in externally verified IDs during admission walk-through.

Allergies verified to lithium are underscoring pen registration. SoAnt allergy note on record and no adverse reactions reported at presentation so far in course of our treatment-still maintained for if need arises as key documentation factor.

No unequal chest rise observed. Effort for breathing is normal and unaffected by possibly lingering lameness or foot pain earlier; chest appears stable and no acute issue has been intimated regardless during monitoring.

Patient in fully safely fastened and stomach! secured and properly onboard for journey continuation with plans in motion for arrival at our receiving ED which was already notified per regulatory field standard for early support, ED staff are aware per landline contact also made by my cell switch number.

Rhythm strip remains in NSR per diagnostic standard query continuously performed and tracking baseline across board. No suspicious rhythms have flagged during the trip while performing 4-lead v CNC while running manually for confirmatory purposes also reviewed in rooms.

No changes in vitals or rhythm detected during any stage of transport-so consistent records and findings in this case today are thus referenced up to ED interface prior beyond which we have nothing to report but clear transmission.

Transportation proceeding on time as intended, arrive before triage threshold estimating time window will still allow for regular flow channeled meeting per patient needs as scheduled for each internalized EPCC protocol page number 2-1 actions.

Patient is in the process of being helped to the hospital bed today via aid of staff. Entry process mobile assisted but no critical incidents anywhere in the process-standard but safe loading for transfer once on ground level in triage area at facility.
Final EKG findings confirm NSR, no acute findings noted-a repeat KTU can show baseline unchanged post this arrival, we already sent EKG paper as physical primary contact here in hand.

Transport team now assisting in offloading patient to receiving stretcher safely and securely-no harm or complications reported while acting here following hospital transport guide directives for glucose-laden EC guidanaces.

Now patient is no longer in inter-facility transport condition as patient is now under hospital license and ED crews have taken over duties-all original documentation has been passed in hand well here as per regulations for EMT team's duties.

No action taken at field level necessitating interventions per protocol, so no additional medications or other modalities used in EMT treatment order beyond initial baseline vitals, ECG and observations pre-hospital move has gone well overall.

ED crew taken over. All on medical team is accounted for per guidelines-responsible personnel are now correct with transfer being complete as per documentation HOLD systems at this point daily procedure captured by gatech EMT software for transparency.
Final vitals before facility arrival: BP 146/92, HR 85, RR 12, SaO₂ 95%, NSR found-handing over per automated documentation triggers for issuing electronic ticket.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: She was brought to the field in ambulatory state but opted for lay flat on cot as per patient request during transport inside vehicle, handled as per transport field protocol of choice.
Patient moved manually from cot to bed as it was feasible and under patient control, no incident occurred during transfer to the bed with no further step needed recorded by either party.

SpO₂ remained constant via transport without drops that required supplemental oxygen or CPAP, which was not utilized during the entirety of our time in the ambulance during any phase to her condition.

Final EKG read as NSR, again with no acute changes-internal documentation field stamp, time residency included as well for completeness in forms to ED supporting their post patient accounting tracking.

Sinus rhythm with possible left atrial anomaly on initial EKG, as a per 12-lead and time stamp at 17:04. Interpretation for clinical ED validation has been passed on again as part of hand HOLD ED staff receiving said data.

Field decision to delay treatment was justified based on clear airway, CTAB, and stable vitals. Ready to hand off as protocol permits to ED who will manage this non-acute, but chest discomfort flagged 8/10 for follow ups recommended after.
No critical changes observed in transit to ED-overall condition of patient has remained the same across entire field-to-hospital run, as outlined in documentation.
Vitals and EKG transmitted to facility receipt at [COMPANY_NAME] as of our prior digital and physical handoff passed off safely for EKG tracing online and noted to have been a key diagnostic tool.

All documentation has been filed as required for field audit tracking through the city trauma registry system. Paper and digital copies filed for duplicates and you can follow via core cast software as ED team confirm for their tracking assignment.
Field protocol applied to chest pain without acute findings as per paramedic rule from 17:09 assessment phases.
Case updated as stable on site, disposition handed as regular triage then subject of regular care as it does within community hospital classification process.

Pain has been noted at 8/10 in initial complaint, but it has since resolved down to a 5/10 intensity per patient verbalization-so we are not doing active reassess at this point due to the change noticed at time of ED handover processing.

Lungs remain clear to auscultation bilaterally and unchanged since initial evaluation during examination shift, as preparations concluded with EMT pretransfer piece of data still open for supportive testing from trauma team also now present here monitoring her with no additional concerns.
Patient's airway intact, within normal limit in transit, vitals under control with no oversight in-school during any portion of trip and our team followed procedures as needed for transport safety.

Vitals and breathing remain stable, ED has recommended triage level under their system at Level 4 non-urgent as per protocol-but we plant to observe and track ID there to ensure adjustments if needed for higher category automatically by receiving staff.
Patient has been transferred carefully and continuously reported to triage ED stretcher as per standard transfer protocols and best practices offered from emergency transport standpoint.

Respirations and pulse remain normal, ED personnel have reviewed all provided information for board inclusion in staging patients queue pending formal classification they will assign for her condition.

Patient herself remains A&Ox2 the entire time with no signs requiring one-on-one monitoring, only basic handoff documented as well on our screens with their log and ED are advised of patient movement checkpoints.

Patient has been aware and informed of estimated arrival using trip time remaining estimates-via updates for better communication about field expectations per protocol for transparency and optimal patient experience.
Left atrial enlargement in initial 12-lead EKG mentioned as possible non-significant finding under note heading, given to ED for evaluation in primary note draft.
Patient's allergy to lithium was confirmed and hand off verb placed for mention in allergic section of patient's EMR or report segments as initial action ED will take.

Patient's trip has occurred with no abnormal movements observed, only breathing noted as early on during presentation but mellowing in-line per serenely monitored patient behavior, as confirmed during field assessment rounds.
ECG tracing has been digitally uploaded as advised for further interpretation by receiving hospital and their cardiologist download per their uploaded assignment mechanism.
